did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Genetic Programming (GP), an evolutionary computation algorithm, for feature selection and feature construction to classify skin cancer (benign vs. malignant) from dermoscopy images. This AI method is applied as part of the experimental methodology to improve the performance of classification algorithms on dermatology data.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing a novel genetic programming (GP) based two-stage approach for feature selection and construction in skin cancer image classification. It details specific methods (LBP, GP, classifiers like SVM, RF, etc.), datasets (LBPgray, LBPrgb), and results (accuracy metrics, statistical tests) to validate the proposed method against existing approaches, which is characteristic of an original research study rather than a review.

title: Genetic Programming for Feature Selection and Feature Construction in Skin Cancer Image Classification
brief_summary: This study develops a novel two-stage genetic programming (GP) approach for feature selection and construction to classify benign versus malignant skin lesions from dermoscopy images. Local binary patterns are used to extract gray and color features, which are then processed by GP to reduce dimensionality and enhance classification performance. The method is evaluated by comparing the performance of common classification algorithms using GP-selected and constructed features against those using the full set of available features. The results demonstrate that the GP-selected and constructed features achieve significantly better or comparable performance in most cases compared to using all features. Furthermore, the analysis reveals that color features have greater potential for distinguishing between benign and malignant lesions than gray-scale features. The evolved feature sets also provide insights into specific skin cancer properties, such as texture patterns associated with streaks and blobs.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used as the malignant class in the binary classification task. Derived from 40 instances in the PH 2 dataset.
Used as the benign class in the binary classification task. Combined 80 common nevi and 80 atypical nevi instances from the PH 2 dataset.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into benign (common nevi, atypical nevi) and malignant (melanoma) classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly frames the task as a 'binary image classification problem' to distinguish between benign and malignant skin cancer images using dermoscopy images. The dataset consists of 200 images from the PH 2 dataset, where 160 images (80 common nevi + 80 atypical nevi) are labeled as 'benign' and 40 images (melanomas) are labeled as 'malignant'. The study evaluates classification algorithms (Naïve Bayes, k-NN, SVM, Decision Trees, Random Forest, MLP) using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic image database gathered at Pedro Hispano Hospital, Portugal, consisting of 8-bit RGB color images used for skin cancer research and benchmarking.

use.training: Used for training classification algorithms (Naïve Bayes, SVM, k-NN, Decision Trees, Random Forest, MLP) with GP-selected and constructed features.
use.testing: Used for testing classification performance (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) of the proposed method compared to using all features.
use.other: The dataset originally contains three classes: common nevi (80 instances), atypical nevi (80 instances), and melanomas (40 instances). For this binary classification study, common nevi and atypical nevi were combined to form the 'benign' class, and melanomas formed the 'malignant' class.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To determine if the performance of GP-selected and constructed features significantly outperformed or was comparable to using the full set of available features.
To report the central tendency and variability of classification accuracy across multiple runs (10 runs) for training and testing sets.
To divide the dataset into training and testing folds to evaluate the performance of the classification algorithms and prevent bias in feature selection/construction.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in the performance (accuracy) between the GP-selected/constructed features and the full set of available features.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in the performance (accuracy) between the GP-selected/constructed features and the full set of available features.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The test was applied to check which feature-set has better ability to discriminate between benign and malignant classes. Symbols '+' and '-' represent significantly better and significantly worse performance, respectively, while '=' represents not significantly different performance of the two feature-sets in comparison with all features.

PH 2.description: Binary classification of benign (common nevi + atypical nevi) vs malignant (melanoma) skin lesions using dermoscopy images. 10-fold cross-validation used for 'All features' baseline. GP-selected and GP-selected-constructed features evaluated over 30 runs with different random seeds.

LBPgray.description: GP selected ~28.26 features on average. Statistical comparison against 'All features' using Wilcoxon signed-rank test (alpha=0.05). Symbols: + (significantly better), - (significantly worse), = (not significantly different).

LBPrgb.description: GP selected ~34.89 features on average. Statistical comparison against 'All features' using Wilcoxon signed-rank test (alpha=0.05). Symbols: + (significantly better), - (significantly worse), = (not significantly different).

PH 2.notes: Color features (LBPrgb) generally showed better performance than gray-scale features (LBPgray). MLP achieved the highest test accuracy of 78.17% ± 0.83 using GP-selected LBPrgb features. GP-selected and constructed features significantly outperformed all features in several cases (indicated by '+') and were comparable ('=') in others, while performing worse ('-') in a few specific cases (e.g., SVM and KNN with LBPgray). The study notes that color features have greater potential for distinguishing benign and malignant lesions than gray-scale features.

architecture_used.main_model: Genetic Programming (GP) for feature selection and construction; standard machine learning classifiers (Naïve Bayes, k-NN, SVM, Decision Trees/J48, Random Forest, MLP) for classification
architecture_used.input_size: 8-bit RGB dermoscopy images; LBPgray features (59 features); LBPrgb features (177 features)

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Local Binary Patterns (LBP) with 3x3 window size and radius of 1 pixel (LBP8,1)

training_details.gp_runs: 10 runs per stage (Stage 1: selection, Stage 2: construction), repeated 30 times with different random seeds

conclusions: The proposed two-stage Genetic Programming (GP) approach for feature selection and construction effectively reduces dimensionality while improving or maintaining classification performance for skin cancer image classification.

GP-selected and GP-selected-constructed features demonstrated significantly better or comparable performance to using the full set of available features in most cases across various classifiers (Naïve Bayes, k-NN, SVM, Decision Trees, Random Forest, MLP).
Color features (LBPrgb) generally showed greater potential and better performance than gray-scale features (LBPgray) for distinguishing between benign and malignant skin lesions.
The Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) classifier achieved the highest test accuracy of 78.17% ± 0.83 using GP-selected LBPrgb features.

The evolved GP feature sets provide interpretable insights into specific skin cancer properties, such as texture patterns associated with streaks and blobs, validating the method's ability to identify discriminative features.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding the small dataset size (PH 2) and the need for future investigation into preprocessing techniques to remove noise, the use of different datasets for generalization, and computation time for real-world clinical application.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a small dataset size (200 images from the PH 2 dataset), which may affect the generalizability of the results.
The dataset is imbalanced, with 160 benign cases (common nevi + atypical nevi) and only 40 malignant cases (melanoma).
The PH 2 dataset was gathered at a single institution (Pedro Hispano Hospital, Portugal), limiting external validation and potential population bias.
The paper acknowledges that preprocessing techniques to remove noise (e.g., hairs, ink, gel bubbles) were not employed, which could impact robustness.
Computation time for real-world clinical application was not fully addressed and is noted as a future investigation area.
The method relies on hand-crafted features (LBP) rather than deep learning, which may have lower performance potential compared to large-scale CNNs but offers interpretability.
Color features (LBPrgb) demonstrated greater potential for distinguishing benign and malignant lesions than gray-scale features (LBPgray).

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized the PH 2 dataset, a dermoscopy image database from Pedro Hispano Hospital, Portugal. The dataset consists of 200 images: 80 common nevi, 80 atypical nevi, and 40 melanomas. For the binary classification task, common and atypical nevi were combined into a 'benign' class (160 images), and melanomas formed the 'malignant' class (40 images). The dataset was used for both training and testing via 10-fold cross-validation. No external or unseen datasets were used for testing.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The paper did not perform cross-dataset validation. All experiments were conducted on the single PH 2 dataset using internal 10-fold cross-validation.

training.counts: 160 images (benign) + 40 images (malignant) = 200 total images; 180 images per fold for training (9 folds)
training.notes: Used for training classification algorithms (NB, k-NN, SVM, J48, RF, MLP) with GP-selected and GP-selected-constructed features. The dataset was split into 10 folds, with 9 folds used for training in each iteration of the cross-validation.

validation.notes: No separate validation set was reported. Model selection for GP feature selection/construction was based on performance on the training folds within the cross-validation loop.

testing.notes: Used for final performance evaluation. In each of the 10 cross-validation iterations, the remaining 1 fold (20 images) was held out as the test set. The test folds remained unseen during the feature selection and construction phases to avoid bias.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: 10-fold cross-validation. In each fold, 9/10 of the data (180 images) was used for training and 1/10 (20 images) for testing. The class ratio was preserved in each fold.
evaluation_protocol.cross_validation_details: 10-fold cross-validation. Folds stayed within the single PH 2 dataset.
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: No unseen external dataset was used. The test folds in the cross-validation were unseen during feature engineering but were part of the same source dataset.

dataset_level_results.model_or_method: GP-selected features + Classifiers (NB, k-NN, SVM, J48, RF, MLP)

metrics.accuracy: Varied by classifier and feature set (see within_dataset_results)

other_metrics.fitness: Balanced Accuracy (BA) used as fitness function for GP: (TP/(TP+FN) + TN/(TN+FP))/2

dataset_level_results.notes: Results are reported as mean accuracy ± standard deviation over 10 folds (for baseline) or 30 runs (for GP methods). Color features (LBPrgb) generally outperformed gray-scale features (LBPgray). MLP with GP-selected LBPrgb features achieved the highest test accuracy of 78.17% ± 0.83.

generalizability_notes: The study is limited by the small dataset size (200 images) and the use of a single dataset (PH 2) from one institution (Pedro Hispano Hospital, Portugal).
The dataset is imbalanced (160 benign vs. 40 malignant).
The paper acknowledges that preprocessing techniques to remove noise (e.g., hairs, ink, gel bubbles) were not employed, which could impact robustness.
The authors note the need for future investigation into using different datasets for generalization.
No external validation was performed, limiting the assessment of external validity.
information_not_reported: No external test set results were reported.
No patient-level separation details were provided (image-level split assumed).
No specific counts for validation sets were reported (none used separately from training/test folds).
No sensitivity/specificity values for the final reported accuracy results were explicitly listed in the main results table, though the fitness function used Balanced Accuracy.
